exercises.title: JSON Serialize and Deserialize
exercises.description: Use json.dumps and json.loads to serialize a Python dict to JSON string and parse it back. Compare the original and restored data, and pretty-print the JSON.
exercises.starterCode:
import json

student = {
    "name": "Alice",
    "age": 20,
    "gpa": 3.9,
    "active": True,
    "courses": ["Python", "Math", "Physics"],
    "address": None
}

# TODO: Serialize student to a JSON string (compact, no indent)
# TODO: Print the JSON string

# TODO: Deserialize the JSON string back to a Python dict
# TODO: Print whether original == restored
# Format: "Data preserved: True"

# TODO: Print a pretty-printed version (indent=2)
exercises.expectedOutput:
{"name": "Alice", "age": 20, "gpa": 3.9, "active": true, "courses": ["Python", "Math", "Physics"], "address": null}
Data preserved: True
{
  "name": "Alice",
  "age": 20,
  "gpa": 3.9,
  "active": true,
  "courses": [
    "Python",
    "Math",
    "Physics"
  ],
  "address": null
}

exercises.title: Parse and Transform JSON Data
exercises.description: Parse a JSON string containing a list of product records. Filter, sort, and summarize the data. Handle missing keys gracefully using .get() with defaults.
exercises.starterCode:
import json

products_json = '''[
  {"id": 1, "name": "Laptop", "price": 999.99, "in_stock": true, "category": "Electronics"},
  {"id": 2, "name": "Desk", "price": 249.99, "in_stock": false, "category": "Furniture"},
  {"id": 3, "name": "Mouse", "price": 29.99, "in_stock": true, "category": "Electronics"},
  {"id": 4, "name": "Chair", "price": 199.99, "in_stock": true, "category": "Furniture"},
  {"id": 5, "name": "Keyboard", "price": 79.99, "in_stock": true}
]'''

# TODO: Parse products_json into a list of dicts

# TODO: Print total products
# Format: "Total products: 5"

# TODO: Filter in-stock products (in_stock == True)
# Print count: "In stock: 4"

# TODO: Sort in-stock products by price descending
# Print top 3: "1. Laptop: $999.99"

# TODO: Print average price of all products
# Format: "Average price: $311.99"
exercises.expectedOutput:
Total products: 5
In stock: 4
1. Laptop: $999.99
2. Chair: $199.99
3. Keyboard: $79.99
Average price: $311.99
hints: in_stock = [p for p in products if p.get('in_stock', False)]
sorted_stock = sorted(in_stock, key=lambda p: p['price'], reverse=True)
for i, p in enumerate(sorted_stock[:3], 1): print(f'{i}. {p["name"]}: ${p["price"]}')
avg = sum(p['price'] for p in products) / len(products)

exercises.title: Custom JSON Encoder for Special Types
exercises.description: Create a custom JSONEncoder that handles datetime objects (serialize to ISO format string) and sets (serialize to sorted list). Test round-trip serialization.
exercises.starterCode:
import json
from datetime import datetime

class AppEncoder(json.JSONEncoder):
    def default(self, obj):
        # TODO: If obj is a datetime, return its isoformat() string
        # TODO: If obj is a set, return sorted(list(obj))
        # TODO: Otherwise call super().default(obj)
        pass


# Test data with special types
event = {
    "title": "Python Workshop",
    "date": datetime(2024, 3, 15, 10, 0, 0),
    "attendees": {"Alice", "Bob", "Carol"},
    "capacity": 30
}

# TODO: Serialize event using AppEncoder
# TODO: Parse back and print each field
json_str = json.dumps(event, cls=AppEncoder, sort_keys=True)
parsed = json.loads(json_str)

print(parsed["attendees"])
print(parsed["capacity"])
print(parsed["date"])
print(parsed["title"])

warmup.title: Quick Recall: JSON Type Mapping
warmup.description: What does this code print? Trace the Python-to-JSON type conversions.
warmup.starterCode:
import json

data = {"x": True, "y": None, "z": [1, 2, 3]}
json_str = json.dumps(data)
print(json_str)

parsed = json.loads(json_str)
print(type(parsed["x"]).__name__)
print(type(parsed["y"]).__name__)
